question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between a planet's distance from the Sun and its orbital velocity?

options.label: A
options.text: Planets further from the Sun orbit at higher velocities because they must travel greater distances

options.label: B
options.text: Planets closer to the Sun orbit at higher velocities because gravitational force is stronger at shorter distances

options.label: C
options.text: All planets orbit at the same velocity regardless of their distance from the Sun

options.label: D
options.text: Orbital velocity is independent of gravitational force and depends only on the planet's mass

correct_answer: Planets closer to the Sun orbit at higher velocities because gravitational force is stronger at shorter distances
explanation: Orbital velocity depends on the balance between gravitational force and the centripetal force needed to keep a planet in orbit. Closer to the Sun, gravity is much stronger (following the inverse square law), so planets must move faster to maintain a stable circular orbit. Mercury, closest to the Sun, orbits at about 47.9 km/s, while Earth orbits at about 30 km/s, and Mars at about 24 km/s. The further away you go, the slower the orbital velocity becomes.
